speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number five. Today's date is February 25. The rescued and aid giver's name is Renee Scott. Miss Scott, we were discussing your arrival in Ravensbruck. Tell me a little bit about the food that you had there.

speaker: interviewee
text: One piece of bread a day. Ersatz coffee with camphor in it every day. Black coffee, never milk or anything. No sugar. Every Saturday we had a little square of ersatz butter that we didn't dare eat, because we thought it was made with human fat. So we'd eat our dry bread. And sometime we couldn't eat the bread because it was rotten. It was all moldy, you know, moldy.

speaker: interviewer
text: But anyway, we had that. Then we had every evening we had rutabaga soup. That's rutabaga is a turnip that they give to the cows, you know, that big turnip? That's what we had every night.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then once every maybe twice a month we had that food for the birds, little grains like, you know. They grow on like-- they grow long like that. Then you take off the little grains. So they'd make that soup. And when we-- we changed the-- the day people would meet the night people. We'd leave the camp-- we'd leave the camp to go back to our barracks. We'd stay in the camp, but I mean we left Siemens factories, for instance. And we had to walk all around the lake to go to our barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'd see the night people, so we'd yell at them, what is it for supper? Every day we'd say that. So they'd-- sometime they'd say, [NON-ENGLISH], that little grain. And we were happy. Oh, we were so happy, because that was a change. We liked it, you know. So that's all we had. Then once about every 15 days or three weeks we had a piece of saus-- salami like, salami, to eat with our bread. Never had vegetable, nothing, just rutabaga. That's why so many people were sick with having turnip, turnip, turnip all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Never-- never anything else. I was dying. I said, if only I had a cracker with a cup of tea. That's what I wanted the most. I think we all did. Excuse me. I hope they don't see me blowing my nose like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: You look fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's what we had to eat. Nothing. But during the appell, you know, when they were counting us, sometimes it would go rather fast, well, say one hour. That was short. That was a short appell. But usually it was sometime maybe one hour, two hours, maybe sometime three hours, sometimes the whole night long. The first Christmas I was there, they put us out naked. It was so cold, because where I-- where Ravensbruck is, it's cold. It's going up towards Russia. And I can tell you it's real cold. They put us out naked the whole night long. The only thing we could do was to stay, you know, one next to the other to try to get a little bit of warmth, one from me and-- and me from her. For no-- because there was one missing in the whole camp. In the whole camp when they counted, there was one person missing. So until they found that person, we had to stay out. That was a horrible-- that only happened once. But that was a horrible thing I'll never forget. How we didn't all die of cold I don't know. You wouldn't believe it. I never had a head cold while I was in Ravensbruck. Never. And now I'm in Boston, I have allergy. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of clothing did you wear while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uniform. Striped uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for your feet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes we had a sandal. But we used to sleep with our sandals under our head, because the other women that didn't have any would steal it from us. So sometime they stole it and you had nothing and you stayed bare feet. Nothing. That's how you get sores in your legs and your feet and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember there being a lot of female SS guards there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Female what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Female guards like you described.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, sure. They were all-- there were as many female than men. And they were wicked. They were terrible. There's one they called-- she was the commander's mistress. They called her the Beauty. She was really beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what she'd do? Now, how I would say. That window's high, right? She would jump to that window and lay on the table in the block, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was wicked. She had a-- like a police leather bat, you know. And she'd land on that table and with her bad she'd go through hitting anybody. She didn't care, anybody she'd hit. She was executed by the British. She was caught at the end of the war. I went to the trial, you know, Nuremberg. She was there. I have a picture. I couldn't show you everything. Anyway, she was amongst all of them, because she was the mistress of the commander. And the British caught her. Well, they caught her. They-- at the trial, they-- I think they were allowed to divide the French had some and the British-- the British had some. And they-- I don't know how they did it. But anyway, she belonged to the English part. And she was executed in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Beauty of Ravensbruck, we called her. I have it there in the pictures. I don't know, don't know. We only called her that-- [FRENCH] we called her. The Beautiful Cow from Ravensbruck, that was the name we gave her. That was even too nice. A cow isn't bad. She was bad. Then we had a man that had a great big ring, about three times that big. He killed the girls. He would kill the prisoners by knocking them here on the temple. He knew exactly where. If he was mad at somebody, he would kill them, like that, right here with his ring and his fist. Ooh, he was mean. We called him [FRENCH], the Black-- the Black Tiger we called him. Oh, he was just cruel. He was caught. I think the Belgiums had him, like Belgium had Baggans too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the selections they had at Ravensbruck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Selections?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Of people. Selections of prisoners.

speaker: interviewee
text: That came to visit us, you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Selections of people to be sent to the chambers or to be killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were so many I wouldn't know. So many that were killed, I couldn't tell you. I know some of my friends-- friends I didn't know their name, but they're my friend. They were a friend. We were all friends. When we were suffering together, we were all friends. I know some of them, they'd come and tell them that they were going to be liberated and they were being executed. But I wouldn't know them by their names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember seeing any of these executions?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I saw men hung in the camp. Our first Christmas, they hung a man and we had to go and walk around him and look at him while we were-- that was our Christmas tree. Poor man. He was all naked and they hung him on a pole like to make a cross. And they put his arms like-- like-- oh, horrible. We had to look at that. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: If I have a nightmare tonight, I'll tell you. Tell me about the camp infirmary.

speaker: interviewee
text: About what?

speaker: interviewer
text: About the infirmary.

speaker: interviewee
text: The infirmary? You-- you went to the infirmary if you had-- like, you what we-- we were very run down. We had no strength, nothing. And when we worked, we had to work in-- when you didn't go to Siemens, you had to work in factories in the camp, right in the camp. And very often you stuck yourself, you know. What they did, they gave you a dirty uniforms that-- where soldiers had died. And they used those uniforms. But they didn't disinfect them or anything. Then the poor prisoners, women prisoners, had to try to sew them, put them together. And they would stick the needle in their finger. And that-- of course, that was infected right away, so they'd go right away to the-- not right away. They'd say they had to go to the infirmary. Sometime they had a finger that was that big, you know. Oh, disgusting looking. There, you went there. So they'd-- if it was real bad, they'd dab it to get the dirt out, you know. I don't know what they'd put on. I didn't have that experience. But they'd put something on it. They had no gauze. They had no bandage. The only thing we-- they used was paper, like tissue paper. Like you'd see all the girls, like me with my legs, they'd put tissue paper around your leg. They had no bandages. Everything went to the war for the soldiers, so we didn't have anything. But the infirmary, they didn't give you anything. You were sick, you were sick. You were sick, well, die. That's all. They didn't care.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned you stayed-- you mentioned you stayed healthy there for a little while and then you became sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was healthy. I was sick when I got there still. Yeah. They took me away from prison. I-- I told you I had a cold. Then I had pleurisy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wasn't too well and I got there at night. It was cold and I got cold again. There I started to be sick the first few weeks that I was in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I got better. And then I had to work. So I worked hard and all that. Then I started to be very sick. I had-- first time, I had otitis in both ears, very bad otitis. They didn't care about that. They didn't do anything. Just stay that way. Then there was one prisoner. She was from [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh, near Poland somewhere. She-- beautiful girl. She was a very good nurse. And she liked-- she got a liking to me. So she said, Mick, I'm going to see if I can bring you something. But she never could get to bring me something. So she said, I'll see what I can do in the future days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, one time then I got-- I got better after that otitis, a little bit better. And then I worked again. It was in the springtime. I worked doing all kinds of things, digging up trenches and trees and wheelbarrow stuff and all that. During the summertime, I didn't mind it too too much, you know. Well, I-- the others either.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then after that I started to get-- be real sick. And I came out with all the black spots that I had typhus. I had the [INAUDIBLE] typhus for five months. I never had an aspirin, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then this-- this nurse-- where was she from? A little country between Poland-- Czechoslovak. And she was such a wonderful girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: She came in one night, everything was quiet. And she said, Mick, I'm bringing you something. And I was even afraid to take it. I thought she's going to probably kill me, get rid of me, you know. And she made me take it and my fever started to go down. But that was towards about my fourth month being sick. But my fever went down and I'm sure she saved me. She saved me. I never saw her again. Never saw her again to thank her ever. But I had that I had ever-- I had pleurisy. I had pneumonia. I had everything in the camp. And I never had an aspirin. You wonder how you survive, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever remember having any contact with any gypsies?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Never. They were there, but we-- we had no contact with them. They would come to our barrack, you know, because they had a bunch of kids. They always had kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they wanted to see if we had no bread to give them or something like that. But we had no-- we weren't allowed to. But they'd come anyway. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we had no contact with them. Were you aware of the gypsies receiving any kind of special treatment, either good or bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: The gypsies, that they had bad treatment? Oh, yeah. They hated them. They hated the gypsies. If they caught one near the barracks, oh my goodness. I think they would have killed them. They would have. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we'd always tell them go. Go away. We have nothing. Go, you know. Did you ever have any encounters with any doctors there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. Thank goodness, no. Only when they-- when they dabbed me in my breast to sterilize me. Tell Tell me about that. Well, one day they had-- a Gestapo came. He had the list, names and the numbers. So he called out 19151. I thought, my goodness. What's going to happen now? You never could tell them you were sick for this or that, because you were afraid, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, he told me I had to come. I got on the line. There were other ones too. I wasn't alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us to the infirmary. And he didn't-- they didn't even put a little alcohol, nothing. That-- come out with a big needle like that. I was afraid. That I dare say, I was frightened. And they dabbed me once here and once here. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They put-- took tissue paper and put it there with a piece of tape. That was it. I'm surprised that it got better, you know, that there was no infection or any-- nothing. But I must say-- and I thank my parents that I had a good health before I was arrested. I was a very strong person. I had been well fed and well taken care of.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point, when you have arrived in Ravensbruck and while you were there, were you aware of what was going on with the gassing?

speaker: interviewee
text: With what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of what was happening to the Jews with the gassing and the ovens?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were aware of what's going on with the Jews and in the world, because we made clandestine-- 'clandan-steen?' How do you say that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Clandestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Made-- we made radios and everything. On top of the block there was like a false attic. And we would get up there.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'd steal stuff from Siemens, wires and electrical things. And we'd bring it back to the block. We'd hide it underneath our-- on our, our-- on our flesh, you know, on our skin, so not make any bumps or anything, because they would come and feel you, you know. If they saw that you had a little bump, they'd come and feel you and say, what, what do you got there? We tried to steal anything we could, you know. So anyway, we'd bring it back. And the girls that had notion to make electrical things, they would. And I'll always remember the time Roosevelt spoke and we had the whole thing. Oh, were we happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: They couldn't make us keep quiet at the appell, and they were wondering why we were so happy, why we were happy. They didn't know what we were doing. They never had the idea. They were intelligent, but they weren't intelligent enough that time to know that we were making radios up there. We were so happy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were always telling me, oh, you know, Mick, we're going-- what's the matter? Nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. You know, Mick, we're gonna-- we're gonna die here. I said, no. I said, this-- we're now in the spring. I said some time this year, we're going to go home. And oh, they said, in French, [FRENCH]. You're crazy. I said, no. You're going to see. We're going to go home. And sure enough, that was in the spring. And towards May, April, May, they started to you know-- we could see something was changing. They weren't-- they didn't dare be quite so cruel in the appells and things like that. And then we would see the Red Cross would come. And they'd give-- oh, they would give packages for us, but we never got them. They ate them. They-- they took them. We wouldn't get them. And then less-- less Jewish people were coming in. Less Jewish girls were coming-- not coming in as much. There weren't so many left either, poor things, you know. But there were less people coming in. And they'd stop the convoys of Belgium and French were not coming in. We said, there's something going on here. I said, I told you we were going to go home. Sure enough, the Red Cross was coming in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Scott, tell me about-- tell me about your work in the Siemens factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. They were working with little precision things. We were all at a bench, windows here, and a long bench and table here. That's where that French girl that blew up the-- that cinema in Paris, she was next to me. And we had little tiny things, small like-- like my little nail there. And we had to work with these precision things and make long ones. So we'd have put them together. And when they were almost together, we'd break them. But watched that he wasn't in back of us, because he was going in back and looking to see what we were doing. Otherwise, we would have been punished. We did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: The first time they put me in Siemens I was in a room like, and I had to break rocks all night long. Break rocks. I said, this is Siemens? So the next day, the guard was nice that was there. He said, this is not for you. You can't do this. You have to do something else. So he brought me to the chef, one of the chef at Siemens. And he said, let me see your hands. So I showed him my hand. He said, what did you do in life? I said, nothing. I was a ballerina.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, oh, that's good. He said, we need hands like that here. That's before I worked with the precision.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, you have nice dry hands and you have little fingers. We want you. So he put me at the benches. And I stayed there until the liberation, not working, but breaking. I broke a lot. And I wasn't caught, thank God. What happened to the girls who were caught?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the girls who were caught?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were punished terribly, beaten, beaten up, beaten. That's all they knew was to beat and hurt. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware that there was a crematorium in Furstenberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a crematorium and there was a-- everything you wanted there, torture rooms, everything. de Gaulle's-- de Gaulle's niece, she was with me. President de Gaulle? She was with me, Genevieve de Gaulle. She was in the block right next to me. I'd see her every day. She was sentenced to death too. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was a good underground worker. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So at Siemens, that's about what I did. I always worked with precision for submarines, for airplanes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Nothing else until the liberation. Did they build a crematorium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they build a crematorium--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, of course they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at Ravensbruck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, huge one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can't-- I never went in the door. I don't know. It just-- it was just a brick thing with iron doors, great big iron doors. And you could smell it. Night and day it was burning and burning and burning. They just-- they came up-- like an oven, big ovens. And they pulled that thing out. They put the body on it and inside it went. We saw it. That we saw it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when they built it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was built before I got there. No. That's been there a long time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Ravensbruck still goes back to Ravensbruck. The people that I gave you the little paper there, they-- every year they go, you know, to respect Ravensbruck. So they're always asking me to go. But it's a long trip for me, first from here to Belgium and then Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Tell me about some other incidents at Ravensbruck that we haven't spoken about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Well, Ravensbruck was built on top of water apparently. I-- I don't-- I suppose it's true, because there were great big-- on the ground, there were great big like you'd say iron-- like you'd say doors. But they weren't doors. It was like a plate, you know, like a plate, a flat plate. And apparently-- they, they always told us, if anything happens, we will flood the camp. And they would open up those things and let the water come out. So very often when they were mad at us, they would tell us they were going to-- at night that they would flood the camp and we couldn't get out. We'd drown. I didn't see it. That I didn't see. But I mean, things-- incidents, we had all kind-- like in the summertime, it was so warm there. In the winter, we froze to death. In the summer, it was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So lots of people couldn't stand the heat and they'd faint. We weren't even allowed to pick them up. You could not help anybody, neither from the cold nor the heat. Just leave them there. They forbid us to touch them, anybody. No help. And then what happened to the girls after that, the ones who had fallen down?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when the appell was over, you went. I don't know what happened to them. I don't know what they did. We didn't know them all, you know. They were all people that were being counted like I was, but I didn't know them all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a number of SS guards in your barrack over the three years you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I have--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have many guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: oh, We always had lots of guards. We had women and men both. We had permanent women all the time. And then the men would come in and-- with dogs and make a round like, you know, make rounds to see if we're keeping quiet or if we're doing anything. I do remember one thing though. I didn't tell you. It's at-- towards the end, you know, when they were-- we could feel things were going bad. And I had no shoes. And the-- the roads in the camp, some were up. Some were bumpy. Some were flat and-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, they decided that they were going to make us-- and there was-- what do you call the ashes from the coal? Coal ashes, you know. They put that on-- on the ground. And bare feet, we had to run on that, going up a road. So when you got at the top, there were the Gestapo there. If he did like that, that meant you were going to stay there. If he went like that, you could continue that way. It was good sign. So the girls told me, please, Mick, do your best. I was so weak. I had had typhus so long. Do your best, Mick. Try to get up that hill and try to go right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I did. But I had said a long prayer. I said, please, God, make me go the right side, the right side. And I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know what happened? I got up at the hill, the top of the hill. And I was so happy, so happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't find my strength, but I ran. And I ran to the first barrack. There's no window. There are windows, but no pane. I jumped into there. And that's where they found me sitting down against a wall, the girls looking for me, prisoners. And they found me sitting there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said, Mick, what are you doing there? I said, I don't know. I probably fell asleep. I must-- or I fainted. I don't know what happened. I was so weak.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, after-- pardon me. After that time, that's when after that they did that experience. Then the appell was only one day, instead of three. Oh, I'm sorry. And they-- they started by coming and telling you, you don't have to go to work today. You can stay there. We knew there was something going on. We knew Roosevelt had done something good for us, for everybody. [DRINKS] Excuse me. So from that time on, as I said, the Red Cross was coming, little more frequent-- frequently. And they would bring vitamin pills, which we never saw either. They would take them. But the Germans took them for them. The officer [INAUDIBLE] and the-- you know, the officers keep them for their families, not for us. But anyway, we didn't care. We said, as long as they're bringing stuff in, that's good sign.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Scott, we're going to change tapes now.
